title: Risk Allocation and Liability
description: Learn how engineering contracts identify, allocate and manage project risks, and how contractual liability may arise from delay, defects, negligence, non-compliance and other failures.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Engineering projects involve technical, commercial, legal and operational risks.
Risk allocation determines which party is responsible when a particular event occurs.
Liability concerns the legal or contractual responsibility for loss, damage or failure.
Clear risk allocation helps parties price their obligations and manage the project effectively.
Poorly allocated or unclear risks frequently lead to disputes, delays and unexpected costs.

sections.title: What Is Project Risk?
content: A project risk is an uncertain event or condition that may affect project objectives.
Risks may affect cost, schedule, quality, safety, performance or reputation.
Some risks are threats, while others may create opportunities.
Risk cannot normally be eliminated completely.
The objective is to identify, allocate and manage it appropriately.

sections.title: Common Engineering Project Risks
content: Typical risks include design errors, delayed delivery, cost increases and equipment failure.
Projects may also face site conditions, regulatory changes, safety incidents and environmental damage.
Other risks include supplier insolvency, labor shortages, currency movements and political instability.
The relevant risks depend on the project's location, complexity, technology and contract structure.

sections.title: What Is Risk Allocation?
content: Risk allocation is the process of assigning responsibility for identified risks.
A contract may allocate a risk to the client, contractor, supplier or another party.
Some risks are shared between several parties.
The party carrying a risk may be responsible for its financial and practical consequences.
Risk allocation is one of the most important commercial functions of a contract.

sections.title: Principle of Efficient Risk Allocation
content: A risk should generally be allocated to the party best able to control or manage it.
The responsible party should also be able to assess and price the risk.
Allocating an uncontrollable risk to a weaker party may increase prices without improving risk management.
Efficient allocation supports realistic pricing and better project outcomes.
However, bargaining power often influences the final allocation.

sections.title: Risk Allocation and Pricing
content: Suppliers normally include the cost of accepted risks in their price.
A contractor carrying significant uncertainty may add a risk premium.
The more unpredictable the exposure, the larger the premium may become.
Transferring every risk to the contractor does not necessarily reduce the client's total cost.
The client may ultimately pay for the transferred risk through higher tender prices.

sections.title: What Is Liability?
content: Liability is responsibility for the consequences of an act, omission or contractual failure.
Liability may arise under a contract, legislation or general legal principles.
A liable party may be required to pay compensation, correct defective work or perform another remedy.
The existence and extent of liability depend on the applicable law and contract wording.

sections.title: Contractual Liability
content: Contractual liability arises when a party fails to perform an obligation under the agreement.
Examples include late delivery, defective design, failure to meet performance guarantees or non-payment.
The contract normally defines available remedies and procedures.
The injured party may need to prove the breach and resulting loss.

sections.title: Liability Outside the Contract
content: A party may also face liability outside the contract.
This may arise from negligence, property damage, personal injury or violation of statutory duties.
Some legal duties cannot be excluded through contract wording.
A project event may create both contractual and non-contractual claims.

sections.title: Breach of Contract
content: A breach occurs when a party does not perform according to the contract.
The breach may involve non-performance, defective performance or late performance.
Not every breach automatically gives the same remedy.
The consequences depend on the seriousness of the breach, the contract and applicable law.

sections.title: Causation
content: A claimant normally needs to show a connection between the breach and the claimed loss.
This connection is known as causation.
A party is not usually liable for every problem that happens after a breach.
The loss must have been caused, at least sufficiently, by the relevant failure.
Complex engineering projects may involve several contributing causes.

sections.title: Foreseeability
content: Compensation may be limited to losses that were reasonably foreseeable when the contract was formed.
Unusual or indirect consequences may be harder to recover.
Parties should communicate special risks before signing the contract.
Clear communication can make unusual potential losses more foreseeable.

sections.title: Duty to Mitigate Loss
content: A party suffering loss is normally expected to take reasonable steps to reduce it.
This is often called the duty to mitigate.
A client should not allow avoidable losses to continue merely to increase a claim.
Reasonable mitigation costs may themselves be recoverable.
Mitigation does not require a party to take unreasonable or unsafe action.

sections.title: Direct and Indirect Loss
content: Contracts often distinguish between direct and indirect or consequential loss.
Direct loss usually follows naturally from the contractual failure.
Indirect loss may involve more remote commercial consequences.
The precise meaning varies between legal systems.
Contracts should define excluded loss categories as clearly as possible.

sections.title: Examples of Potential Loss
content: Direct costs may include repair, replacement, rework and additional labor.
Delay may create extra site costs, storage expenses or extended equipment rental.
Commercial losses may include lost production, lost profit or loss of business opportunity.
Environmental incidents may create cleanup costs, fines and third-party claims.
The contract may treat these categories differently.

sections.title: Limitation of Liability
content: A limitation of liability clause restricts the amount or type of liability a party may face.
The contract may establish an overall financial cap.
It may also create separate caps for delay, warranty or specific risks.
Limitations allow parties to assess their maximum contractual exposure.
They must be drafted clearly and may be restricted by mandatory law.

sections.title: Liability Caps
content: A liability cap may be expressed as a fixed amount or percentage of the contract value.
The cap may apply to all claims combined or to individual claims.
Parties should clarify whether damages, indemnities and other remedies fall within the cap.
A low cap may leave the client exposed to losses that cannot be recovered.

sections.title: Exclusions from the Liability Cap
content: Some liabilities may be excluded from the general cap.
Examples may include fraud, deliberate misconduct, confidentiality breaches and intellectual property infringement.
Personal injury or death may also be treated separately.
The treatment depends on the agreement and applicable law.
Exclusions should be expressly identified.

sections.title: Exclusion Clauses
content: An exclusion clause removes liability for specific categories of loss.
A common example is exclusion of indirect or consequential loss.
Other clauses may exclude loss of profit, production or revenue.
The wording must be sufficiently precise.
Ambiguous exclusions may be interpreted narrowly.

sections.title: Liquidated Damages
content: Liquidated damages are predetermined amounts payable for a specified breach.
They are commonly used for delay or failure to meet performance targets.
The amount may be calculated per day or week of delay.
They provide predictability and reduce the need to prove the exact loss.
The clause must comply with the applicable legal rules.

sections.title: Delay Liquidated Damages
content: Delay liquidated damages compensate the client for late completion or delivery.
The contract should define the completion date and the triggering event.
It should also define the applicable rate and maximum amount.
The contractor may avoid liability when entitled to an extension of time.
Accurate schedule records are essential in delay disputes.

sections.title: Performance Liquidated Damages
content: Performance liquidated damages may apply when equipment fails to achieve guaranteed performance.
Examples include insufficient output, excessive energy consumption or reduced efficiency.
The contract should define the testing method and acceptance criteria.
It should also state whether payment allows the client to accept reduced performance.

sections.title: Indemnities
content: An indemnity is a contractual promise to protect another party against specified loss or claims.
Indemnities are often used for third-party injury, property damage and intellectual property claims.
The clause should identify the covered events and losses.
It should also explain claim notification, defense and settlement procedures.
Indemnity wording can create significant exposure and should be reviewed carefully.

sections.title: Third-Party Claims
content: Engineering activities may cause loss to persons who are not parties to the contract.
Examples include injury to visitors or damage to neighboring property.
The contract may allocate responsibility for handling such claims.
Insurance and indemnity provisions often operate together.

sections.title: Negligence
content: Negligence generally involves failure to exercise the required level of care.
Engineers and contractors are expected to act with reasonable professional competence.
The applicable standard of care depends on the role, circumstances and legal system.
A poor outcome alone does not always prove negligence.
The conduct must be evaluated against the required standard.

sections.title: Professional Standard of Care
content: Designers are often required to exercise reasonable skill and care.
This is different from guaranteeing a perfect result.
The professional may be liable if the work falls below the expected competence level.
The contract should avoid creating unintended obligations that exceed available insurance.

sections.title: Fitness for Purpose
content: A fitness-for-purpose obligation requires the completed work to achieve a defined purpose or result.
This may be stricter than a reasonable-skill-and-care obligation.
A contractor may be liable even when it acted carefully if the required result is not achieved.
Such obligations should be clearly identified and priced.
They may not be fully covered by professional indemnity insurance.

sections.title: Design Responsibility
content: The contract should clearly allocate responsibility for design.
The client may provide the design, or the contractor may design all or part of the project.
Responsibility can become unclear when both parties contribute.
Review or approval by the client does not automatically transfer design responsibility.
Interfaces between designers should be carefully managed.

sections.title: Defects
content: A defect is work or equipment that does not satisfy the contract requirements.
Defects may relate to design, material, workmanship or performance.
The contract normally gives the contractor an opportunity to correct defects.
The client may obtain other remedies if correction is not completed.

sections.title: Defects Liability Period
content: Many contracts include a defined period for reporting and correcting defects.
This is commonly called a defects liability or notification period.
The contractor remains responsible for covered defects during that period.
The period does not necessarily eliminate liability for hidden defects discovered later.

sections.title: Warranties
content: A warranty is a contractual promise concerning quality, condition or performance.
Warranties may cover materials, workmanship and equipment reliability.
The contract should define the warranty period and available remedies.
Supplier warranties should be coordinated with the contractor's obligations to the client.

sections.title: Client-Caused Risk
content: The client may cause delay or additional cost through late information, access restrictions or design changes.
The contractor may be entitled to additional time or payment.
The entitlement depends on notice requirements and contractual procedures.
Failure to provide timely notice may weaken the contractor's claim.

sections.title: Contractor-Caused Risk
content: The contractor normally carries risks related to its methods, workforce, suppliers and coordination.
Poor planning or subcontractor failure may remain the contractor's responsibility.
The contractor should manage these risks through planning, supervision and suitable subcontracts.

sections.title: Site Condition Risk
content: Unexpected ground or site conditions can create major cost and schedule impacts.
The contract should state which party carries this risk.
The client may provide site information, but its accuracy may be qualified.
Contractors should review available information and perform required investigations.
Notice procedures are important when unexpected conditions are found.

sections.title: Force Majeure
content: Force majeure clauses address exceptional events outside the parties' reasonable control.
Examples may include natural disasters, war, embargoes or government restrictions.
The clause should define covered events and available relief.
Relief may include additional time, suspension or termination.
Additional payment is not always available.

sections.title: Insurance
content: Insurance transfers certain financial risks to an insurer.
Common project policies include property, construction, liability and professional indemnity insurance.
Insurance does not replace contractual risk management.
A contract may allocate risks that are excluded from available insurance.
Parties should review coverage, exclusions, deductibles and policy limits.

sections.title: Relationship Between Liability and Insurance
content: A party may remain liable even when insurance does not respond.
Contractual liability should therefore be compared with actual insurance coverage.
Uninsured exposure may create serious financial risk.
The contract should not assume that every liability is insurable.

sections.title: Subcontractor Liability
content: The main contractor is often responsible to the client for subcontractor performance.
The client normally does not need to pursue the subcontractor directly.
The main contract and subcontracts should contain compatible obligations.
Liability gaps may arise when subcontractor caps are lower than the main contractor's exposure.

sections.title: Back-to-Back Contracting
content: Back-to-back contracting aligns subcontract obligations with the main contract.
The subcontractor should accept relevant technical, schedule and liability requirements.
Simply stating that a subcontract is back-to-back may be insufficient.
Important obligations should be incorporated clearly and specifically.

sections.title: Risk Register
content: A risk register records identified risks and planned responses.
It may include probability, impact, owner, mitigation action and current status.
The register should reflect contractual risk allocation.
It should be updated throughout the project.

sections.title: Risk Responses
content: Common responses include avoiding, reducing, transferring and accepting risk.
Avoidance removes the activity creating the risk.
Reduction lowers probability or impact.
Transfer allocates financial consequences to another party or insurer.
Acceptance means the party retains and manages the risk.

sections.title: Notice Requirements
content: Contracts often require formal notice when a risk event occurs.
The notice may need to be submitted within a specified period.
It should describe the event, expected impact and requested relief.
Failure to comply may reduce or eliminate contractual entitlement.
Project teams should maintain a clear notice procedure.

sections.title: Documentation and Evidence
content: Liability claims depend heavily on reliable evidence.
Useful records include correspondence, meeting minutes, schedules, photographs and inspection reports.
Cost records and time sheets may be needed to prove financial impact.
Poor documentation can prevent a valid claim from succeeding.

sections.title: Practical Scenario: Late Information
content: The client delivers critical design information four weeks late.
The contractor's work is delayed and additional labor costs arise.
The contractor should review the contract, issue timely notice and document the impact.
The client may carry the risk if the information was its responsibility.

sections.title: Practical Scenario: Design Failure
content: A contractor designs a system that fails to achieve the guaranteed output.
The contractor argues that it followed accepted engineering practices.
If the contract contains a fitness-for-purpose obligation, reasonable care may not be a complete defense.
The exact liability depends on the wording of the performance obligation.

sections.title: Practical Scenario: Subcontractor Delay
content: A subcontractor delivers critical equipment late.
The main contractor misses the contractual completion date.
The main contractor may remain liable to the client.
It must then determine whether it can recover the loss under the subcontract.

sections.title: Practical Scenario: Unexpected Ground Conditions
content: Excavation reveals conditions that were not shown in the site information.
The contractor should stop affected work where necessary and notify the client.
The parties should determine who carries the risk under the contract.
The contractor should document the actual condition and resulting impact.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Allocating every risk to the contractor without assessing who can manage it.
Accepting unlimited liability without understanding the exposure.
Confusing insurance coverage with contractual protection.
Failing to align subcontracts with the main contract.
Missing notice deadlines.
Using unclear exclusions or indemnities.
Failing to document delay, defects or additional costs.

sections.title: Best Practices
content: Identify key risks before contract award.
Allocate each risk to the party best able to manage it.
Use clear and specific contract wording.
Review liability caps and exclusions carefully.
Compare contractual exposure with insurance coverage.
Align main contracts and subcontracts.
Follow notice procedures.
Maintain complete project records.
Update the risk register throughout execution.

sections.title: Role of the Project Manager
content: The project manager should understand the project's main risk allocations.
The manager coordinates technical, commercial and legal responses to risk events.
Potential claims should be identified early.
Relevant notices, records and mitigation actions should be managed promptly.
Specialist legal or insurance advice should be obtained when necessary.

sections.title: Summary
content: Risk allocation determines which party carries the consequences of uncertain events.
Liability arises when a party is legally or contractually responsible for loss or failure.
Effective allocation considers control, knowledge, pricing and insurance.
Contracts may limit liability through caps, exclusions and liquidated damages.
Indemnities, warranties and insurance also influence exposure.
Clear wording, timely notices and strong documentation are essential.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Allocate risks to the party best able to manage them.
Understand the difference between risk and liability.
Review liability caps, exclusions and indemnities.
Distinguish reasonable skill and care from fitness for purpose.
Ensure subcontract obligations support the main contract.
Check whether contractual risks are insured.
Follow notice requirements carefully.
Document causes, consequences and mitigation actions.
Do not assume that transferring risk always reduces total project cost.
